why_correct: Assistant prefilling exploits the autoregressive constraint: Claude cannot contradict tokens already committed in the assistant turn, so opening with a raw bash command forces all subsequent tokens to continue that trajectory. This is a structural enforcement mechanism, not an instruction-following one — it removes the failure mode entirely rather than asking Claude to comply harder. The correct pattern when output format must be machine-consumed without post-processing is always structural constraint over natural language instruction, because instructions operate probabilistically while prefill operates deterministically.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is that you're still relying on instruction-following to solve what is fundamentally an instruction-following failure. Stronger system prompt language shifts the probability distribution but doesn't eliminate preamble — under load, novel phrasing, or edge-case context windows, the model can still drift. A would be correct if prefilling weren't available and you were tuning for reduced occurrence rather than elimination.
distractors.C: This is the temperature misconception — conflating stochastic variation with behavioral mode. Preamble generation isn't high-temperature noise; it's a learned conversational pattern that appears even at temperature 0. In production, lowering temperature here gives you false confidence while the failure mode persists deterministically on certain prompt shapes.
distractors.D: Regex post-processing moves the trust boundary in the wrong direction — you're now writing fragile pattern-matching logic to compensate for nondeterministic model output, and every novel preamble format Claude invents becomes a new production incident.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for machine-consumed output is: if a human instruction can be violated, it will be — use structural constraints instead. Prefilling is the correct tool whenever the output must begin in a specific format, because it converts a probabilistic instruction into a deterministic continuation constraint.
cross_domain_note: This pattern matters in agentic loop design too — prefilling the assistant turn with a partial JSON object or function call structure is the same mechanism used to enforce reliable tool-use output schemas when the orchestration layer has zero tolerance for malformed responses.

why_correct: The Anthropic API contract is explicit: the stop sequence token is consumed as the halt trigger but excluded from the returned content field. The response terminates at the last generated token before the matched sequence, so the raw content is already clean at the boundary. Adding a strip call introduces a latent correctness risk — if a valid bash block legitimately ends with backtick-adjacent syntax, the strip corrupts the payload. The correct architectural pattern is to trust the API contract and validate structure, not re-sanitize boundaries the API already guarantees.

distractors.B: The failure mode here is treating a stable API contract as a runtime variable. Stop sequence exclusion is not model-version-dependent behavior — it's a documented API guarantee at the request/response layer, not an inference-time heuristic. Defensive code against a guaranteed contract is not 'sound'; it's noise that obscures real failure modes and can introduce data corruption when the 'defensive' trim touches valid content.
distractors.C: This is the partial-match misconception — the idea that multi-character stop sequences might be partially emitted before the halt triggers. That's not how stop sequence matching works: the API buffers until the full sequence is matched, then halts and strips the entire matched token before returning. There's no partial-inclusion edge case for multi-character sequences; the contract is all-or-nothing.
distractors.D: Replacing a precise stop-sequence boundary with a broad markdown-fence regex trades a well-defined contract for an ambiguous scrubbing rule. This is the over-sanitization anti-pattern — it looks defensive but breaks on any bash block whose content contains fence syntax, and it conflates output boundary control with content normalization, which are separate concerns.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: stop sequences are consumed, not echoed. If your post-processing strips what the API already excludes, you're either writing dead code or introducing a corruption vector on valid edge-case content. Trust the API contract at the boundary; apply validation logic to structure and semantics, not to boundary characters the API owns.
cross_domain_note: This same contract-trust principle applies to prompt caching — architects sometimes add redundant cache-invalidation logic because they distrust cache hit guarantees, producing the same anti-pattern: extra complexity that can corrupt behavior while solving a problem the platform already handles.

why_correct: Prefill-plus-stop-sequence shifts extraction from probabilistic pattern matching to deterministic boundary enforcement at the protocol level. By injecting the opening fence as the assistant turn prefix, you guarantee the generation starts exactly where you declare — Claude cannot emit prose before it because you've already written that token. The stop sequence then halts generation at the closing fence, so the returned payload is the command text and nothing else, no regex, no parsing surface, no model-update brittleness. The extraction boundary is a hard constraint imposed on the generation, not inferred from its output.

distractors.A: JSON mode is the right tool when your downstream consumers can accept a JSON envelope — but the stem explicitly requires no downstream system changes, which rules it out structurally. The failure mode isn't wrong architecture in isolation; it's wrong architecture given the constraint. If the downstream executors were being redesigned simultaneously, A becomes competitive.
distractors.B: This is the 'strengthen the regex' anti-pattern — it looks like a fix but just adds more brittle surface area. Every anchoring heuristic you add is a new assumption about model output phrasing, and the stem already tells you model updates shift that phrasing. In production, this breaks on the next model update in a different way, still silently, still reaching executors. You've increased maintenance cost without changing the failure class.
distractors.D: Negative-constraint prompt engineering has no enforcement mechanism — 'never include prose' is a soft instruction that degrades under temperature variance, long contexts, and model updates. The failure mode is silent non-compliance: the model occasionally includes a comment or changes its fence style, and you're back to unpredictable output with no structural guarantee.

mental_model: When extraction must be resilient to model output drift, move the boundary declaration upstream into the generation protocol rather than parsing it downstream from the result. Prefill-plus-stop-sequence is the only extraction strategy where the contract is enforced before tokens are sampled, not after. If you're writing a regex to extract structured output, you've already lost the architectural argument.
cross_domain_note: This same principle applies in agentic pipelines: tool-call schemas enforce output structure at the protocol layer the same way stop sequences do — both avoid putting a parsing assumption downstream of probabilistic generation. Recognizing that pattern generalizes the heuristic across function-calling architectures.

why_correct: Stop sequences are deterministic — they fire when the exact token sequence is emitted, full stop. The prefill-plus-stop-sequence pattern's failure mode at scale is almost always max_tokens exhaustion: the generation hits the token ceiling before the closing fence, producing a truncated but structurally intact prefix. At 0.3% failure rate across three format types with different average output lengths (bash commands are short, SQL can be long, YAML can be arbitrarily deep), the correct diagnostic is to audit per-endpoint token budgets and right-size them, not to indict the extraction pattern itself.

distractors.A: Stop sequences don't fire probabilistically — they're string-match triggers on the token stream, not a threshold crossing. The misconception conflates temperature-driven output variability with stop sequence mechanics. A non-deterministic stop sequence would produce truncations at random positions; what you actually see with max_tokens exhaustion is truncation always at the token limit, which is the diagnostic tell the SRE lead is missing.
distractors.C: JSON mode constrains output format but doesn't solve token exhaustion — if max_tokens is too low, JSON mode will produce a partial JSON object just as readily as prefill-plus-stop-sequence produces a partial command. Schema validation at the application layer will then reject the malformed JSON, so you've traded a partial bash command for a validation exception with no reduction in failure rate. JSON mode is the right choice when you need nested structured data with variable schema, not as a fix for budget misconfiguration.
distractors.D: Prefill tokens do count against the context window, but the prefill strings here are a handful of tokens (e.g., '```bash\n') — they're not meaningfully competing with command generation budget. The failure rate won't respond proportionally to trimming three tokens off a prefill when the real issue is that SQL queries can run 300+ tokens and the token budget wasn't calibrated per endpoint.

mental_model: When you see partial outputs in a prefill-plus-stop-sequence pipeline, your first diagnostic is always token budget, not stop sequence reliability. Stop sequences are deterministic; budgets are misconfigured. Segment the failure rate by format type — if SQL failures outpace bash failures, max_tokens is almost certainly your culprit.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to cost optimization: right-sizing max_tokens per endpoint also reduces average token spend per call, which at 50k daily calls is a material billing lever worth surfacing to the platform team alongside the reliability fix.

why_correct: Prefilling the assistant turn removes the interpretation step entirely — Claude isn't being asked to follow a format directive, it's already mid-output and continues from established state. When the assistant turn opens with 'SELECT' or a SQL block delimiter, there is no surface for a conversational opener to appear because the output has already begun. This is the fundamental architectural distinction: instructions describe desired behavior probabilistically, while prefill constrains the output space mechanistically. For any pipeline with zero tolerance for format drift, prefill is the correct control plane.

distractors.A: Emphasis and repetition increase compliance probability but don't eliminate the variance — you're still in instruction-following territory, not output-state territory. In production, emphatic instructions help with systematic non-compliance but fail intermittently under the exact same conditions that caused the original 1-in-12 failure rate. The failure mode is that you've reduced the probability without changing the underlying mechanism.
distractors.C: Temperature governs sampling stochasticity across the token distribution, but conversational openers like 'Sure, here is the query:' aren't low-probability tokens in this context — they're high-probability assistant-turn initializers. Lowering temperature can actually increase the likelihood of the most common assistant opening pattern. This is the classic misconception that non-compliance is a temperature problem rather than a prompt architecture problem.
distractors.D: Splitting the constraint into the system prompt is a cleaner separation of concerns than A, but it's still an instruction-following approach. The format rule now lives in a higher-trust position, which helps with deliberate override scenarios, but doesn't eliminate the probabilistic gap between 'Claude read the rule' and 'Claude's first token is SQL.' The architectural tell in the stem is 'pipes directly into a query executor' — that's a zero-tolerance signal that rules out any instruction-based approach.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for format-critical pipelines: if downstream has zero tolerance for structural deviation, instructions are insufficient by definition — use prefill to constrain the output's initial state rather than directing behavior. Any time you see 'intermittent parse failures' in a stem, that's the signal that you've exceeded what instruction-following can reliably guarantee.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to trust boundary design in agentic pipelines — prefill is a form of output-layer enforcement analogous to output schema validation in tool-use responses, where you constrain the response structure at the protocol level rather than relying on the model to self-enforce.

why_correct: stop_sequences is a hard generation halt — the moment Claude emits the matched token sequence, the API closes the response stream. With stop_sequences=['```'] and prefill '```bash', Claude generates the bash block body and then hits the closing fence, which terminates generation entirely; the YAML block is never entered into the token stream at all. The correct pattern when you need N distinct structured artifacts is N independent API calls, each with its own prefill and stop sequence, giving you clean parse boundaries and independent retry/routing logic per consumer.

distractors.A: The near-miss intuition is that you can chain stop sequences to 'step through' multiple blocks, but that's not how the parameter works. Adding '```yaml' as a second stop sequence doesn't change the fact that generation already halted at the first '```' match — you'd need the first call to have already produced the YAML block for a second stop sequence to be relevant. This option would be correct only if stop_sequences were an ordered delimiter list, which it isn't.
distractors.C: This conflates prefill specificity with generation continuity. The language hint in '```bash' vs '```' has no bearing on whether Claude continues past the first closing fence — the stop sequence is the termination signal regardless of what prefill you use. In production, swapping to a generic prefill just removes the language tag and still halts at the first matched fence.
distractors.D: HTTP 200 with an empty YAML payload rules out a router race condition — if the block existed in the raw response body, a buffer fix would be testable and observable. The architectural tell is 'consistently receives empty payloads' across all calls; a race condition produces intermittent failures, not systematic ones. The failure is deterministic and upstream.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: stop_sequences is a generation kill switch, not a delimiter iterator. Any design that expects Claude to produce multiple sequentially parseable blocks in one response with a shared stop sequence is broken by construction — one stop fires, stream ends, everything after it is void. One artifact per API call is the invariant.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic loop design: orchestration layers that fan out to multiple tool consumers should issue independent model calls per tool payload, not attempt to parse a single response into multiple tool inputs. The same 'one call, one artifact' principle prevents ambiguous function call extraction in multi-tool scenarios.

why_correct: stop_sequences is a one-shot termination mechanism: generation halts permanently at the first matched sequence in the output stream, full stop. There is no pause-and-resume semantics — the API call is over the moment any entry in the list matches. The correct pattern for extracting N distinct structured artifacts is N distinct API calls, each with its own prefill and stop sequence targeting that artifact's closing delimiter. Trying to multiplex multiple artifacts into one generation turn fundamentally misunderstands the contract of the parameter.

distractors.A: The failure mode is treating stop_sequences like a stateful checkpoint system. It is not ordered, and it does not pause — it terminates. A would be correct if the API supported a streaming-with-resume or multi-segment extraction protocol, but no such mechanism exists in the Claude API. Adding '```json' just means you now have two possible early-exit triggers, not two extraction windows.
distractors.C: This is the near-miss because reordering prefill is a real lever in structured extraction — but it only matters when you want to capture the *first* artifact. The core error remains: one API call with stop_sequences still terminates at the first match, regardless of which artifact is prefilled first. You'd capture IAM JSON and lose Terraform, not gain both.
distractors.D: max_tokens controls output budget, not extraction logic. The Terraform block is present in 100% of responses, which rules out a token-budget exhaustion root cause — if max_tokens were the constraint, you'd see truncated output, not cleanly complete single-artifact responses.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: stop_sequences = eject button, not chapter marker. Any time a design requires extracting N artifacts from one generation turn using stop sequences, the correct architecture is N API calls. One generation turn, one artifact — that's the invariant.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic loop design: multi-step tool use pipelines that try to collapse sequential extraction steps into a single Claude turn for latency savings hit the same wall. Prefill-plus-stop is a single-artifact primitive; orchestration across artifacts belongs in the orchestration layer, not inside one inference call.

why_correct: The prefill pattern works by injecting tokens directly into the assistant turn, so generation begins mid-stream inside the code fence — there is literally no position in the token sequence where prose preamble can appear before the stop sequence fires. A user message instruction, by contrast, is a directive that Claude interprets and then begins a fresh assistant turn, creating a structural gap between instruction acknowledgment and format compliance. The stop sequence only terminates generation; it cannot prevent tokens from being emitted before the stop boundary is reached. In a high-volume pipeline, that compliance gap is where intermittent failures originate — Claude occasionally emits 'Here is the SQL:' before the fence, the stop sequence fires after the damage is done, and the downstream parser breaks.

distractors.A: System prompt directives do carry strong instruction weight, but they still leave the structural gap intact — Claude begins a new assistant turn and can emit prose before reaching the format-compliant token. A is right in a low-stakes or interactive context where near-compliance is acceptable, but it fails the specific constraint here: zero-tolerance parse failures in high-volume production. The architectural tell is 'intermittent non-compliance causes downstream parse failures' — that rules out any solution that still allows preamble tokens to appear.
distractors.C: This confuses inference cost mechanics with structural compliance. Prefill doesn't reduce prompt token count — the injected assistant turn actually adds tokens. The argument collapses immediately under token accounting. The real cost savings from prefill come from cache hit potential on repeated system prompts, not from format instruction re-derivation, which isn't a meaningful compute concern at the per-call level.
distractors.D: Benchmark compliance rates don't hold under production distribution shift — edge cases, unusual schema names, or slightly ambiguous queries erode that rate. More critically, even a 99% compliance rate means 1-in-100 calls produce a parse failure, which at high volume is a continuous incident. Arguing that the stop sequence is 'reliable enough' also misunderstands the failure mode: the stop sequence fires correctly, but prose before the fence already corrupted the payload.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for structured extraction pipelines: prefill doesn't instruct Claude to comply, it removes the structural opportunity to non-comply. Any solution that preserves a fresh-assistant-turn boundary — system prompt, user message, or strong instruction weight — still owns the compliance gap. When parse failures are intolerable, eliminate the gap architecturally, don't negotiate it down probabilistically.
cross_domain_note: This same prefill-eliminates-gap logic applies in agentic tool-use pipelines: prefilling the assistant turn with a partial function call JSON structure removes the window where Claude might emit a reasoning preamble before the tool call, which breaks orchestration layers that expect raw function call syntax as the first token.

why_correct: Stop sequences are matched against the raw token stream as Claude generates it — they fire on any occurrence of the target string in the response body, not just at semantically meaningful positions like closing fences. The heredoc inline comment literally contains '```', so that match triggers mid-script, and the API returns whatever was generated up to that point. Since the pipeline has zero post-processing before handing the response to the executor, the truncated shell script runs as-is, leaving teardown in a partially applied state — exactly the worst-case failure mode for infrastructure mutation pipelines.

distractors.A: The prefill content is not re-scanned for stop sequence matches — it's treated as already-consumed context, not newly generated tokens. Stop sequence matching only applies to tokens Claude produces after the prefill boundary. A would be correct if the API re-evaluated the entire context including prefill, but that's not the mechanism.
distractors.C: This inverts the actual prefill behavior. The prefill string does not appear in the API response; Claude continues generation from that point without echoing it back. But stop sequence evaluation still applies to everything Claude generates after the prefill — the closing fence Claude produces would still trigger the stop, and so would any inline occurrence like the heredoc comment. The 'omits closing fence' conclusion doesn't follow from the prefill mechanics.
distractors.D: The truncation originates at the API layer, not the executor. The shell never sees a complete heredoc to misinterpret — it receives a structurally incomplete script because the response was cut short before the stop sequence review would even matter.

mental_model: Stop sequences are dumb string matchers against the generation stream — they have no semantic awareness of code structure, comment context, or nesting depth. Any pipeline that uses a stop sequence to delimit structured output must either sanitize the content domain for that string or add a post-processing layer that validates structural completeness before passing output downstream. The architectural tell in this stem is 'no post-processing' — that's the load-bearing detail that turns a latent stop-sequence collision into a live infrastructure incident.
cross_domain_note: This is a direct trust boundary failure: the pipeline implicitly trusts that generated content will never contain the stop sentinel, which is an assumption that breaks under realistic content variance. The same class of bug appears in prompt injection defense — any delimiter-based trust boundary that the content domain can accidentally or intentionally emit is not a real boundary.
